Du kan få din mann til å komme til menigheten, selv om han klager og ikke vil gå. Bare be. Ikke prøv å tvinge det fram, for da vil det ikke fungere. Men hvis du virkelig har kjærlighet for hans sjel i hjertet ditt, vil han merke det. Ikke bekymre deg. Mannen blir helliget av den troende kona, og omvendt. Du kan ikke late som. Du kan ikke bløffe. Det må virkelig være der. Det er grunnen til at ekte tro... Folk tror noen ganger at de har tro når de egentlig ikke har det. Du må virkelig ha den. Jeg har sett den samme seirende kraften.

text:
As you know, I'm a lover of outdoors; and love the wilds, raised in the woods. My mother about a half Indian. And I love the wild. I was game warden in Indiana for years, studied wildlife, lived in it all my days. My old granddad was a noted national-known hunter. And I've hunted all over the world nearly. I love wildlife.
But, I tell you, there's something about genuineness. There's something about "real" that everybody will know. You won't have to go around testifying this, that or the other. If you're a real lover of the Lord, everybody you come in contact will almost know it. You are written epistles. You are sealed. And a seal is on the backside the same as on the front side, both going and coming. People know that you're a Christian. You walk different, live different, act different---you are different---when you become a Christian.
I remember, one time (I might have told you this.) how that I have seen wildlife conquered by the love of God. How many ever read my book called Man Sent From God, was wrote here by Brother Lindsay. Many have.
text_no:
Som du vet, elsker jeg friluftsliv og er glad i villmarken, oppvokst i skogen. Min mor var delvis indianer, og jeg elsker naturen. Jeg var viltvokter i Indiana i mange år, studerte dyreliv og har levd i det hele mitt liv. Min gamle bestefar var en kjent nasjonaljegermester, og jeg har jaktet nesten over hele verden. Jeg har en stor kjærlighet til dyrelivet.
Men jeg forteller deg, det er noe med ekthet. Det er noe med å være ekte som alle vil gjenkjenne. Du trenger ikke vitne om dette eller hint. Hvis du er en virkelig elsker av Herren, vil nesten alle du kommer i kontakt med vite det. Du er levende epistler. Du er forseglet, og en segl er på baksiden så vel som på forsiden, både når du går og når du kommer. Folk vet at du er en kristen. Du går annerledes, lever annerledes, oppfører deg annerledes—du er annerledes—når du blir en kristen.
Jeg husker en gang (jeg kan ha fortalt deg dette tidligere) hvordan jeg har sett dyreliv overvunnet av Guds kjærlighet. Hvor mange har noen gang lest boken min kalt "Mannen sendt fra Gud," skrevet her av Bror Lindsay? Mange har.

text:
You remember the maniac that fell on the platform that night across my feet? You remember that story? What happened? I wished I could explain that. I've had it happen many times in my life. I have it to happen every time that a miracle is performed. It's something. You have to enter in.
Here a few weeks ago, Brother Jack and I were standing on the platform before thousands of thousands of people, down in Mexico. And a poor old Mexican brother come up. He was blind. And he had a shawl laying over him---dirty, dusty. And the poor old fellow had a little cross in his hands, to say a "Hail Mary," or something. I told him that wasn't necessary.
So he come up. And he wanted to know… Come up to where I was. I looked at the old fellow. I seen his gray hair from under his big old straw hat, where he had pulled it down. And he was saying something in Spanish. I couldn't understand him. Brother Espinoza was telling me what he was saying. And he come over to where I was at. And he put his arms upon my shoulders. Something struck me. I looked at him. About seventy. I said, "That would be about the age of my old dad, if he was living."
text_no:
Husker du den maniske mannen som falt på plattformen den kvelden, rett ved mine føtter? Husker du den historien? Hva skjedde? Jeg skulle ønske jeg kunne forklare det. Det har skjedd mange ganger i mitt liv. Det skjer hver gang et mirakel utføres. Det er noe spesielt. Man må inngå i det.
For noen uker siden stod Bror Jack og jeg på plattformen foran tusenvis av mennesker, nede i Mexico. En stakkars gammel meksikansk bror kom opp. Han var blind og hadde en skitten, støvete sjal over seg. Den gamle mannen holdt et lite kors i hendene, muligens for å si en "Hail Mary" eller noe. Jeg sa til ham at det ikke var nødvendig.
Så kom han opp til der jeg var. Jeg så på den gamle mannen. Jeg så hans grå hår under den store, gamle stråhatten som han hadde trukket ned. Han sa noe på spansk som jeg ikke forstod. Bror Espinoza fortalte meg hva han sa. Den gamle mannen la armene sine på skuldrene mine. Noe traff meg. Jeg så på ham; han var rundt sytti år. Jeg sa: "Det ville være omtrent alderen på min gamle far, hvis han fortsatt levde."

text:
I looked at his feet. Didn't have on any shoes. I thought, "Maybe, he never had a pair of shoes."
All of a sudden, Something begin to move me. I put my foot up beside of his, to see if my shoes would've fit him. I thought, "If my shoes fit me, I'll take them right off now and give them to him. He maybe, never had a pair of shoes." I thought, "Here he is." I pulled him up close to me to see if my shoulders and his… He was wider. I'd give him my coat. I loved him. Something another, I loved him. I thought, "Look at there. There the poor old fellow, maybe, never sat down to a good decent meal in his life. Maybe he never wore a pair of shoes," his old rusty, scaly-looking feet, and his toe nails all turned up. I thought, "Poor old fellow. He's got just as much right to a good pair of shoes as I got. Got just as much right to wear a good coat as I can. Got just as good a right … as much right to sit down to a good meal as I have."
text_no:
Jeg så på føttene hans. Han hadde ikke på noen sko. Jeg tenkte: "Kanskje han aldri har hatt et par sko."
Plutselig begynte noe å bevege meg. Jeg satte foten min ved siden av hans for å se om mine sko ville passe ham. Jeg tenkte: "Hvis skoene mine passer ham, tar jeg dem av nå og gir dem til ham. Han har kanskje aldri hatt et par sko." Jeg tenkte: "Her er han." Jeg trakk ham nærmere meg for å se om skuldrene våre var like brede, men han var bredere. Jeg ville gi ham jakken min. Jeg elsket ham. Noe i meg elsket ham. Jeg tenkte: "Se der. Den stakkars gamle mannen har kanskje aldri satt seg ned til et skikkelig måltid i livet sitt. Kanskje han aldri har hatt et par sko." Hans gamle, rustne, skjellaktige føtter og neglene hans som bøyde seg oppover. Jeg tenkte: "Stakkars gamle mann. Han har like mye rett på et godt par sko som jeg har. Like mye rett til å bære en god jakke som jeg. Like mye rett til å sette seg ned til et godt måltid som jeg."

text:
And besides all that, the devil has blinded him. See what it was? Somehow, I entered in… Not me! The Holy Spirit, (O God!) was taking me in to the feeling. Oh, when you can project your life, by taking me into a feeling of love for the old man!
And directly he put he put his arms over my shoulders and begin weeping on my shoulder. Then, something happened. I thought, "That cursed, blind devil, that's blinded this poor old man, that's never had any privileges."
"Why," I said, "You blind spirit, come out of him."
And all of a sudden, he opened his eyes and he begin to scream, "I can see." What was it? Projecting the love. How the Holy Spirit… That's the way it happens.
When that poor man run to the platform to take my life that night, threatened he would do it, rolled up his big arms before sixty-five hundred people besides the hundreds standing in the streets in the rain. He said, "You hypocrite. Tonight I'll break every bone in your body."
I never said a word. I looked at him. I weighed a hundred and twenty-eight pounds and him nearly three hundred, seven-foot-something tall. Maniac out of asylum. And he run up there and he…
text_no:
I tillegg til alt dette, har djevelen gjort ham blind. Forstår du hva det var? På en eller annen måte gikk jeg inn... Ikke meg! Den Hellige Ånd, (Å Gud!), førte meg inn i følelsen. Åh, når du kan projisere ditt liv ved å lede meg inn i en følelse av kjærlighet for den gamle mannen!
Han la direkte armene rundt skuldrene mine og begynte å gråte på min skulder. Da skjedde noe. Jeg tenkte: "Den forbannede, blinde djevelen som har gjort denne stakkars gamle mannen blind og fratatt ham alle privilegier."
Jeg sa: "Du blinde ånd, kom ut av ham."
Med ett åpnet han øynene og begynte å rope: "Jeg kan se!" Hva var det? Det var projeksjonen av kjærlighet. Hvordan Den Hellige Ånd... Slik skjer det.
Da den stakkars mannen løp opp på plattformen den kvelden, truet han med å drepe meg. Han rullet opp de store armene sine foran 6500 mennesker, i tillegg til hundrevis som sto ute i regnet. Han sa: "Din hykler. I kveld skal jeg knuse hvert bein i din kropp."
Jeg sa ikke et ord. Jeg så på ham. Jeg veide 58 kilo, mens han var nesten 136 kilo og over to meter høy. Han var en galning, rett ut fra asylet. Han løp opp til meg, og han...

text:
Now, instead of… Well, I was scared, sure. But, when I looked at him, something begin to happen. Instead, of thinking, "Oh, if I was big enough, I'd whip you." Instead of that, I got to thinking, "Poor brother. The devil has got him bound. Why, he'd want to love me as much as I love him. He can't help being like that. That's not him cursing me. That's the devil." That is with any man. It's not the man that's cursing you, that you want to get angry with and kill him in a few minutes. It's the devil making him do that. Not your brother. It's the devil he's possessed with.
And he made a great big threat. And before I could say anything, the Holy Spirit begin to speak. And instead of hating the man, I loved him. And the love of God was projected to him, and he rolled around his big eyes like that, and fell on my feet in the floor. Love conquered.
text_no:
I stedet for å bli redd, skjedde noe annet da jeg så på ham. I stedet for å tenke: "Å, hvis jeg var stor nok, ville jeg slått deg," begynte jeg å tenke: "Stakkars bror. Djevelen har bundet ham. Han ville elsket meg like mye som jeg elsker ham hvis han kunne. Han kan ikke for det; det er ikke han som forbanner meg, men djevelen." Dette gjelder enhver mann. Det er ikke mannen som forbanner deg som du skal bli sint på og ønske å drepe. Det er djevelen som får ham til å gjøre det, ikke din bror. Det er djevelen som har besatt ham.
Han kom med en stor trussel, og før jeg rakk å si noe, begynte Den Hellige Ånd å tale. I stedet for å hate mannen, elsket jeg ham. Guds kjærlighet ble projisert mot ham, og han sperret opp de store øynene sine og falt på gulvet ved mine føtter. Kjærligheten seiret.

text:
I've heard them talk about bad dogs. It happens to be I'm not afraid of a dog. What makes the dog bite you is because you're afraid of him. Now that may sound silly, but it's not. Wild animals … I never seen one I was afraid of, because I love them. I've been face to face with grizzly bears and everything else, because I love the animals. You have to have it; you can't bluff it now. Did you ever see a dog raise his old … and go… He knows whether you're afraid or not. Don't you try to bluff him. You might have bluffed your neighbor, but you can't bluff the dog. He knows. And you can't bluff God. And I say this with all reverence: You can't bluff the devil. I don't care how loud you holler, how much you scream, and kick your feet. He'll just lay right there.
But when you really got the article, you don't have to say very much. He knows whether it's real or not. Them disciples was screaming and hollering, trying to make this lunatic well. Jesus said, "Come out of him." What happened? The disciples were defeated. But that devil had to know where that voice was coming from. It was from a fountain were the Dove of God was sitting. He recognized that vibration when it went from Him. He knew that was more than a disciple. He knew that was more than Moses. When he tempted Moses, and he made Moses sin… But when he said, "If Thou be the Son of God, make a miracle here before me, show me."
He said, "It's written, 'Man shall not live by bread alone.'" He knowed he hadn't met Moses then. There was something about it. Right.
text_no:
Jeg har hørt dem snakke om farlige hunder. Tilfeldigvis er jeg ikke redd for en hund. Det som gjør at hunden biter deg, er fordi du er redd for ham. Det kan høres rart ut, men det er sant. Ville dyr … Jeg har aldri sett et dyr jeg var redd for, fordi jeg elsker dem. Jeg har stått ansikt til ansikt med grizzlybjørner og andre dyr, fordi jeg elsker dem. Man må virkelig ha denne kjærligheten; man kan ikke bløffe seg til den. Har du noen gang sett en hund reise seg og begynne å knurre? Den vet om du er redd eller ikke. Ikke prøv å bløffe den. Du kan kanskje bløffe naboen din, men ikke hunden. Den vet det. Og du kan ikke bløffe Gud. Jeg sier dette med all respekt: Du kan ikke bløffe djevelen. Jeg bryr meg ikke om hvor høyt du skriker eller hvor mye du sparker og skriker. Han blir bare liggende der.
Men når du virkelig har den rette troen, trenger du ikke si så mye. Djevelen vet om det er ekte eller ikke. Disiplene skrek og ropte for å gjøre denne gale mannen frisk. Jesus sa, "Kom ut av ham." Hva skjedde? Disiplene ble beseiret. Men djevelen måtte vite hvor den stemmen kom fra. Det var fra en kilde hvor Guds Due satt. Han gjenkjente den vibrasjonen når den gikk fra Ham. Han visste at dette var mer enn en disippel. Han visste at dette var mer enn Moses. Når han fristet Moses og fikk Moses til å synde … Men når han sa, "Hvis Du er Guds Sønn, gjør et mirakel her foran meg, vis det for meg."
Jesus svarte, "Det står skrevet, 'Mennesket lever ikke av brød alene.'" Djevelen visste at han ikke hadde møtt Moses da. Det var noe spesielt med Jesus. Riktig.

text:
Here sometime ago, while I was game warden in Indiana, over at the old Burks Farm, where the old quarry is, where the one at … Walnut Ridge graveyard, just below where my dear loving wife is resting tonight---her body, her and the baby---right down in there, it was called the Burk's Farm; there was a big old bull. And he killed a colored man down there. And they'd sold him up there to a fellow named Guernsey, this side of Henryville. I was letting some fish loose down there, in a big lake where a steam went through it. This fellow made a lake in there and he was going open it up to the public, and we stocked it for him---the conservation.
Well, we're supposed to pack a little old pistol around wherever we went. That was a law, we had to do it. So, I happened to know of a brother across the field, that was sick. So, I thought, "I believe I will run over to pray for him. I'll turn these fish loose."
I unbuckled this little old pistol and throwed it up in the car, and went on over there where the … started across the field where the brother was. I forgot that that killer was in the field.
So, on my road across the field, over, going across that way, just here a little bunch of shrubs out there, little scrub oaks. And I happened to get about two hundred yards, or three hundred from the fence, about the same distance the other side, little swamp of a pond in the middle there. And out … and all of a sudden within thirty feet of me, this killer bull raised up. And there I was.
text_no:
Her for en tid siden, mens jeg var viltvokter i Indiana, ved den gamle Burks-gården, hvor det gamle steinbruddet er, og hvor Walnut Ridge-kirkegården ligger—rett nedenfor der min kjære, elskede kone hviler i natt—hennes kropp, henne og babyen—rett der nede, ble stedet kalt Burk's Farm. Der var det en stor gammel okse som drepte en farget mann. De hadde solgt den til en kar ved navn Guernsey, på denne siden av Henryville. Jeg skulle slippe ut noen fisk i en stor innsjø hvor en bekk gikk gjennom. Denne fyren hadde laget en innsjø der og skulle åpne den for publikum, og vi fylte den med fisk for ham—bevaringsmyndigheten.
Vi var pålagt å bære en liten pistol rundt uansett hvor vi gikk. Det var lov, vi måtte gjøre det. Jeg visste at en bror på den andre siden av åkeren var syk, så jeg tenkte: "Jeg tror jeg vil løpe over for å be for ham. Jeg slipper ut disse fiskene først." Jeg løsnet denne lille pistolen og kastet den opp i bilen, og gikk videre over der broren var. Jeg glemte at drapsoksen var i åkeren.
På vei over åkeren, mens jeg gikk den veien, var det bare noen små busker og lavvokste eiketrær. Jeg hadde gått omtrent to hundre eller tre hundre meter fra gjerdet og like langt til den andre siden, med en liten sumpdam midt imellom, da plutselig, bare tretti fot unna meg, reiste denne drapsoksen seg. Og der sto jeg.

text:
First, I reached for the gun. No gun. See, how God makes everything just right? No gun. I looked to the fence… He was too close to me. No tree to get into. Death laid before me. He was a killer. Warnings all around, "Don't enter here." Well, I forgot about it. And there I was.
Well, there was only one thing for me to do: straighten myself up and don't die like a coward; die like a man. I straightened myself up, I thought, "Well, this is the end of it." I pulled myself up. No need of trying to run; he was in twenty feet of me. Oh, just throwed his horns in the ground, like that, and bellering with all he could, fall on his knees, and throw his horns, and kicking the dirt, and getting ready.
So, I was just standing there, that's all I could do. I thought, "Well, I certainly will do this: I'll not go down a coward. I'll go down showing my love of God in my heart." And I looked back at that bull, and all of a sudden, something happened. Now, this sounds kiddish, it sounds like a little baby talk. But that's what's the trouble of us tonight: We try to be an adult in God when we ought to be babies in God. We know so much. God dwells in the center of humility and simplicity. Don't never forget that. I looked at the bull; if anything, I could have cried for him. Why, I thought, "Poor creature. Well, how wrong I am." Now ordinarily, I wouldn't feel that way about it.
text_no:
Jeg strakte meg etter pistolen. Ingen pistol. Ser du hvordan Gud ordner alt? Ingen pistol. Jeg så mot gjerdet... Han var for nærme meg. Ingen tre å klatre i. Døden lå foran meg. Han var en morder. Advarsler overalt: "Ikke gå inn her." Vel, jeg hadde glemt det. Og der var jeg.
Det var bare én ting å gjøre: rette meg opp og ikke dø som en feiging, men som en mann. Jeg rettet meg opp og tenkte: "Dette er slutten." Jeg strammet meg opp. Ingen vits i å prøve å rømme; han var innen tjue fots avstand. Han kastet hornene i bakken, brølte med all sin kraft, falt på knærne og kastet hornene, sparket opp jord og gjorde seg klar.
Så, jeg sto bare der, det var alt jeg kunne gjøre. Jeg tenkte: "Jeg vil i det minste gjøre dette: Jeg vil ikke gå ned som en feiging. Jeg vil vise Guds kjærlighet i mitt hjerte." Jeg så på oksen, og plutselig skjedde noe. Nå høres dette barnslig ut, som små barns prat. Men det er vårt problem i kveld: Vi prøver å være voksne i Gud når vi burde være barn i Gud. Vi tror vi vet så mye. Gud bor i sentrum av ydmykhet og enkelhet. Glem aldri det. Jeg så på oksen; om noe, kunne jeg ha grått for ham. Jeg tenkte: "Stakkars skapning. Hvor feil jeg har tatt." Normalt ville jeg ikke følt det slik.

text:
First, I was looking for the gun. I was glad I didn't have him; I'd have shot him, and then went and paid for it. But I didn't have the gun. I thought, "Well, poor creature. Why, I'm in your pasture, this is your home and you're just a brute. You don't know any different. And you wouldn't want to kill me anyhow. But, I disturbed you. I'm sorry I did that. I didn't mean to disturb you." Talking like I am now. I just couldn't help it, there was Something talking in me.
Oh, my! It's too bad, we ever have to leave that. Just submit it. God just took over. And the bull roaring and going on. He lowered his head. And I said, "Now creature of God, I'm the servant of God. I'm on my way to pray for a sick man, over here dying. And I come across the field here, where your home is. I'm sorry I broke into your home." That's what I did. I broke into his home. That's all the home he knows of. Now, now, stop and think, that's true.
I said, "I come into your house. I broke in on you. I'm sorry. You forgive me. I'll go across the field; I won't bother you." I said, "Now, in the name of Jesus Christ, our Creator, go over there and lay down again. I'll not bother you." And that bull took towards me as hard as he could. And when he got in about six or eight feet of me, I wasn't a bit more afraid than I am standing right here.
"Perfect love casts out all fear."
I don't care, if death is staring you in the face, you can sing: Happy day, happy day, since Jesus washed my sins away.
text_no:
Først lette jeg etter pistolen. Jeg var glad jeg ikke hadde den, for da ville jeg ha skutt ham og senere betalt for det. Men jeg hadde ikke pistolen. Jeg tenkte: "Vel, stakkars skapning. Jeg er i din innhegning, dette er ditt hjem, og du er bare et dyr. Du vet ikke bedre, og du ønsker ikke å drepe meg. Men jeg forstyrret deg. Jeg beklager at jeg gjorde det. Jeg mente ikke å forstyrre deg." Jeg snakket slik jeg gjør nå. Jeg kunne ikke hjelpe det, det var Noe som snakket i meg.
Å, herlighet! Det er synd vi noen gang må forlate det. Bare overgi deg. Gud tok over. Oksen brølte og fortsatte. Han senket hodet. Jeg sa: "Nå, Guds skapning, jeg er Guds tjener. Jeg er på vei for å be for en syk mann som ligger her og dør. Jeg kommer over feltet her, som er ditt hjem. Jeg beklager at jeg brøt inn i ditt hjem." Det var det jeg gjorde. Jeg brøt inn i hans hjem. Det er alt hjemmet han vet om. Nå, tenk nøye, det er sant.
Jeg sa: "Jeg kom inn i ditt hus. Jeg brøt inn på deg. Jeg er lei meg. Tilgi meg. Jeg skal krysse feltet; jeg skal ikke plage deg." Jeg sa: "Nå, i Jesu Kristi navn, vår Skaper, gå der borte og legg deg igjen. Jeg skal ikke plage deg." Og den oksen løp mot meg så hardt han kunne. Da han var omtrent seks eller åtte meter fra meg, var jeg ikke mer redd enn jeg er nå.
"Fullkommen kjærlighet driver frykten ut."
Jeg bryr meg ikke om døden stirrer deg i ansiktet, du kan synge: "Lykkelige dag, lykkelige dag, siden Jesus vasket mine synder bort."

text:
No wonder, Paul could stand in the face of death, and say, "O death, where is your sting? Grave where is your victory?" Sure. Through tribulations, he had learned the love of God. Suffering brings patience; works, tribulations bring patience, of course. Now patience makes love, trust.
And when that bull got within about six or eight feet of me, he just threw his feet out and stopped. And he looked at me. He looked this way, and that way, so depleted. Just quietened right down, walked over and laid down. I walked within five feet of that bull, and he never even turned and looked at me. I walked right out of the pasture. What is it? It was the love of God. The Power of God.
You say, "Brother Branham, I don't believe that story." Well, you don't have to. But it's true. The same God that could close the mouth of the lion in the lions' den, for Daniel, could certainly tame a bull in a few minutes. When the love of God has been projected, then when I love the Lord, and the love in my heart comes back to that bull, then when love had come to its end, divine, sovereign grace steps in to take the place. Hallelujah! Oh, my. Then I'll not fear when death comes.
How I want to love the Lord, that when it comes to the place where everything is gone, divine grace will step in some day, and pack me across the river of Jordan, yonder, into a better land where there's no sickness, or death, or sorrow. I love the Lord. You love the Lord. We have got nothing, no sickness, no diseases, nothing else can separate us. That love of God goes right on. And when you love Him, and come to the end where love can't act no longer in your behalf, sovereign grace will take love's place. Amen.
text_no:
Paulus kunne stå overfor døden og si: "Død, hvor er din brodd? Grav, hvor er din seier?" Gjennom trengsler hadde han lært å kjenne Guds kjærlighet. Lidelse gir tålmodighet; trengsler gir tålmodighet, naturligvis. Og tålmodighet skaper kjærlighet og tillit.
Da den oksen kom innenfor seks eller åtte fot fra meg, stoppet den brått. Den så på meg, så denne veien og den veien, så tom ut. Den ble bare rolig, gikk bort og la seg ned. Jeg gikk innenfor fem fot av oksen, og den vendte seg ikke engang og så på meg. Jeg gikk rett ut av innhegningen. Hva var det? Det var Guds kjærlighet. Guds kraft.
Du sier kanskje, "Bror Branham, jeg tror ikke på den historien." Vel, det trenger du ikke. Men den er sann. Den samme Gud som stengte munnen på løven i løvehulen for Daniel, kunne definitivt temme en okse på få minutter. Når Guds kjærlighet er projisert, kommer Guds suverene nåde. Hallelujah! Å, min! Da skal jeg ikke frykte når døden kommer.
Hvordan jeg ønsker å elske Herren, slik at når alt annet er borte, vil guddommelig nåde trå til en dag og føre meg over Jordan-elven, til et bedre land der det ikke finnes sykdom, død eller sorg. Jeg elsker Herren. Du elsker Herren. Vi har ingenting å frykte, ingen sykdom, ingen lidelser som kan skille oss. Guds kjærlighet fortsetter. Og når du elsker Ham, og det kommer til et punkt hvor kjærligheten ikke lenger kan handle på dine vegne, vil suveren nåde ta kjærlighetens plass. Amen.

text:
Here sometime ago, out in my front yard, I was mowing with a mowing machine---little old power mower. And I'd mow about two rounds, here come another car load. I'd run around the house, and run in, take off my overalls and put on clothes, and somebody come be prayed for. I'd pray for them, maybe in there an hour or more. Maybe be late evening, I'd slip out, put my overalls on, run out in the front yard, mow a couple more rounds. Why, the front yard was growing up before I could get in the back.
And then one afternoon, back there, I went back there, nobody… I just slipped on my overalls, real hot. I didn't put on any shirt. Nobody could see me, so I was just mowing along there, the little old power mower, putt-putt-putt-putt.
And I was going along, and I forgot that a big nest of hornets was in the corner. And I rammed right into that fence and in less than a second's time, I was completely covered over with hornets. You know what they are? Those great big fellows. Well, they could kill you. One of them can knock you down. And, of course, me with no shirt on. And the hornets all around me. But something happened. Something… Instead of being scared, love come in and took its place. You might not want to believe this, but, at the judgment bar, you'll see Him, when the secrets of our hearts is disclosed.
text_no:
For en tid tilbake, ute i hagen min, klippet jeg gresset med en gammel gressklipper. Etter å ha klippet to runder, kom en bil til. Jeg skyndte meg inn i huset, tok av meg arbeidsklærne og tok på meg vanlige klær, og noen kom for å bli bedt for. Jeg ba for dem, kanskje i en time eller mer. På ettermiddagen snek jeg meg ut igjen, tok på meg arbeidsklærne og klippet et par runder til. Hagen foran huset grodde til før jeg rakk å komme til bakhagen.
En ettermiddag, da det var veldig varmt og ingen så meg, tok jeg på meg arbeidsklærne uten skjorte og begynte å klippe med den gamle gressklipperen. Jeg glemte at det var et stort vepsebol i hjørnet av hagen. Jeg kjørte rett inn i gjerdet, og i løpet av et øyeblikk var jeg dekket av veps. Vet du hva de er? De store vepsene som kan være dødelige. En av dem kan slå deg ned. Jeg hadde ingen skjorte på, og vepsene svermet rundt meg.
Men noe skjedde. I stedet for å bli redd, ble jeg fylt med kjærlighet. Du tror kanskje ikke dette, men på dommens dag vil du se Ham, når hjertets hemmeligheter blir avslørt.

text:
I was covered over. Not a one had stung me. And I thought, "Poor little creatures. Well, that's the only way you have of defense. God gave you that stinger. That's to defend yourself. You've got just as much right… You don't know that was my fence. It's just as much yours as it is mine. It don't belong to either one of us, it's God's. So you made your nest right there. I ought to have been looking out for you. And I'm sorry. But," I said, "I'm the servant of the Lord. And I love you, little fellow."
And they went, "Bzzzz, Bzzzz."
I said, "I---I---I…" Don't you try to bluff it. You better know what you're talking about. I said, "I love you, little creatures of God. I'm sorry, I apologize to you for waking you up. But," I said, "I'm serving God's people. And I have to cut my grass, and I was in a hurry. I'm sorry I disturbed you. Now, in the name of Jesus Christ, go back into that nest. I won't bother you anymore. I'll go around it."
And them still all around me. My mower never stopped. I just turned loose the handle to raise up my hands to God. I took right a hold of the handles, not a bit more scared of one of them hornets than nothing.
And God, my solemn Judge, them hornets made a few rounds around me, and made a beeline, just as straight back into that nest as they could go. What was it? The projecting of love of God, brought down the sovereign grace of God. And somehow, how would I know, that with me speaking in English, they didn't gear down the interpretation, and they heard what I said. I can't tell you, but anyhow, they obeyed the very thing I asked them in Christ's name to do. Love! It conquers animals. Conquers human beings. It's God.
text_no:
Jeg var helt dekket over, men ikke én av dem stakk meg. Jeg tenkte: "Stakkars små skapninger. Vel, det er deres eneste måte å forsvare dere på. Gud ga dere den brodden, den er for å forsvare dere. Dere har like mye rett… Dere visste ikke at det var min hage. Det er like mye deres som det er min. Den tilhører ingen av oss, den tilhører Gud. Så dere lagde reiret deres der. Jeg burde ha vært mer oppmerksom på dere. Og jeg er lei meg. Men," sa jeg, "jeg er Herrens tjener, og jeg elsker dere, små venner."
De summet: "Bzzzz, Bzzzz."
Jeg sa, "Jeg… jeg… jeg…" Ikke prøv å bløffe det. Du bør vite hva du snakker om. Jeg sa, "Jeg elsker dere, små Guds skapninger. Jeg er lei meg, jeg beklager å ha vekket dere. Men," sa jeg, "jeg tjener Guds folk. Jeg måtte klippe plenen, og jeg hadde det travelt. Jeg er lei meg for å ha forstyrret dere. Nå, i Jesu Kristi navn, gå tilbake til reiret. Jeg skal ikke bry dere mer. Jeg skal gå rundt det."
De var fortsatt overalt rundt meg. Gressklipperen min stoppet aldri. Jeg slapp håndtaket for å løfte hendene mine til Gud. Jeg holdt fast igjen i håndtakene, uten den minste frykt for en eneste av de vepsene.
Og Gud, min høytidelige Dommer, de vepsene sirklet rundt meg noen ganger og fløy så rett tilbake til reiret sitt som de kunne. Hva var det? Guds kjærlighet, som brakte ned Guds suverene nåde. Og på en eller annen måte, hvordan skulle jeg vite, at mens jeg snakket på engelsk, overhørte de min melding, og de forsto hva jeg sa. Jeg kan ikke forklare det, men på en eller annen måte adlød de nøyaktig det jeg ba dem om i Kristi navn. Kjærlighet! Den overvinner dyr. Den overvinner mennesker. Det er Gud.

text:
You've read the possum story, all of you did, I guess. Most of you. Here, the boys is right here tonight, Gene and Leo, sitting right over here. I was sitting on the steps, last July, I believe it was, June or July. July? I believe it was July, last July. Mr. Woods had just been mowing my yard; a rake laying in the yard. And I was talking to these boys; I call them my students. And so I was talking to them about a colored girl. The day before (It was in the paper.), a young, beautiful young colored woman, that had an illegitimate baby, and she wrapped it in a blanket, and smothered it to death. And she took it out by cab, and went up there and throwed this blanket in the river, all wrapped up with wire. The cab driver got suspicious. He said, "What'd you throw in there?"
Said, "Oh, just some stuff I didn't want." The cab driver reported it to the police, the police reported it to the Coast Guard, they went and sent it out, and it was a baby. So there it was.
text_no:
De fleste av dere har sikkert hørt historien om pungrotten. De fleste. Her er guttene i kveld, Gene og Leo, som sitter her borte. Jeg satt på trappen i fjor sommer, jeg tror det var i juni eller juli. Juli? Ja, jeg tror det var i juli. Mr. Woods hadde nettopp klippet plenen min, og en rake lå på plenen. Jeg snakket med disse guttene, som jeg kaller mine studenter. Jeg fortalte dem om en farget jente. Dagen før sto det i avisen om en ung, vakker farget kvinne som hadde fått et uekte barn. Hun pakket det i et teppe og kvelte det til døde. Så tok hun en drosje og kastet dette teppet, innpakket i tråd, i elven.
Drosjesjåføren ble mistenksom og spurte: "Hva kastet du i elven?"
Hun svarte: "Bare noe jeg ikke ville ha." Drosjesjåføren meldte fra til politiet, som igjen varslet kystvakten. De undersøkte saken, og det viste seg å være en baby. Så der var det.

text:
And I said, "She wasn't mother." She was just a female. That's right. She wasn't a mother. A mother wouldn't act like that. I said, "She didn't have no mother love in her heart." I said, "She could…" And no more than I'd said it, coming in my gate… I'm the fourth person from a little woods, about a city block away, across a highway and then down a lane. No one along there has a gate but me, and a fence. And here come into my gate, around ten o'clock in the morning, a possum. And she was leaning like this. Well, anyone who knows what a possum is, knows that a possum don't travel in daytime; they're night prowlers. They hunt their prey at night and lay up in the day time. I've trapped them a many time, eat them, many a one, myself. So I know what possums are. So, I seen them coming in.
That's my study of wildlife. I study the nature of them. You see God in wildlife. You see God in nature, everywhere. You see God in men. You see God in children. You see … God is all around you. I watch Him in the birds.
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Hun var ikke en mor." Hun var bare en kvinne. Det stemmer. Hun var ikke en mor. En mor ville ikke oppført seg slik. Jeg sa: "Hun hadde ingen morskjærlighet i sitt hjerte." Jeg sa: "Hun kunne..." Og like etter at jeg hadde sagt det, kom en opossum inn gjennom porten min. Jeg bor som den fjerde personen fra en liten skog, omtrent en byblokk unna, over en hovedvei og ned en sti. Ingen andre langs veien har en port eller et gjerde bortsett fra meg. Og der, rundt klokken ti om morgenen, kom en opossum inn gjennom porten min. Opossumen halte seg fremover. Alle som kjenner til opossumer vet at de ikke beveger seg på dagtid; de er nattdyr. De jakter om natten og hviler om dagen. Jeg har fanget mange opossumer og selv spist en del. Så jeg vet godt hva opossumer er.
Dette er min studie av dyrelivet. Jeg studerer deres natur. Man ser Gud i dyrelivet. Man ser Gud i naturen, overalt. Man ser Gud i mennesker. Man ser Gud i barn. Gud er rundt oss hele tiden. Jeg observerer Ham i fuglene.

text:
Somebody said… My neighbor over there had a radio on. Every time he goes to mow the yard, he has to put a radio on, with that old rock-and-roll, shinny-dig, and boogie-woogie, every kind of stuff they call it. And I said to him one day, I said, "Why, do you put that on?"
He said, "You know what, Billy?" He said, "I can't even mow unless I hear the radio."
I said, "It makes me sick at my stomach."
He said, "Well, if you just put one on one time, and listen at it, what a help it is."
I said, "I always have one on."
He said, "You do?"
I said, "Oh, yes. Every time I start to mowing, my radio comes on."
Said, "I never hear it."
I said, "Oh, you just don't listen to it."
He said, "What is your radio?"
I said, "I get out here and get this old mower started, the mocking birds go to singing, the robins go to whistling. That's the best radio I ever heard in all my life." Right. God sings to me through His birds. O God. What love is. I wished I could somehow have words to tell you.
text_no:
En nabo av meg hadde alltid på radioen. Hver gang han skulle klippe plenen, måtte han ha på radioen med den gamle rock-and-roll, boogie-woogie og lignende. En dag spurte jeg ham: "Hvorfor må du ha på radioen?"
Han svarte: "Du vet hva, Billy? Jeg klarer knapt å klippe plenen uten å høre på radioen."
Jeg sa: "Det gjør meg kvalm."
Han sa: "Om du bare prøvde å ha på en radio en gang, ville du se hvor mye det hjelper."
Jeg svarte: "Jeg har alltid på en radio."
Han sa: "Har du det?"
Jeg sa: "Å ja. Hver gang jeg begynner å klippe, kommer radioen min på."
Han sa: "Jeg hører den aldri."
Jeg sa: "Du legger bare ikke merke til den."
Han spurte: "Hva er din radio?"
Jeg svarte: "Når jeg starter denne gamle gressklipperen, begynner spottefuglene å synge, og rødstrupefuglene begynner å plystre. Det er den beste radioen jeg noen gang har hørt." Ja, Gud synger til meg gjennom Sine fugler. O Gud, hvilken kjærlighet! Jeg skulle ønske jeg hadde ord til å forklare deg dette.

text:
This old possum come on in. I said, "Look at there boys, that possum's got rabies." I run out there real quick. I said, "I better stop her." Coming right to me. And I grabbed this rake, and threw it over her, I noticed her leg on the left side here, was just about, oh, about that big around. Three or four times the size of a possum's leg. The dogs had either got over her and chewed her, or either she had been hit by a car. And there was maggots all over her, where she had done rottening, gangrene all set in, flies, green flies all over her. And I said, "Oh, here's what it is, it's hurt. It hasn't got rabies. I had it under the rake then, the big yard rake, laying down. And Mr. Woods come up across the field. Just then, Mr. Gilmore, the milkman, come in the gate.
I said, "Now, boys,"---Gene and Leo, here---I said, "come here. I want to show you something. This possum…" And I happened to look. And a possum and a kangaroo is the only two animals that has the pocket that they carry their babies in. And so, she happened to letting down, and when her nerves hit her, like that, when I put the rake over her, she let down, and when she let her pocket loose, there were nine little naked possums, about like that, little bitty fellows, that she had in this pocket. Well, as soon as she let down, them little fellows were trying to nurse, and her biting at that rake.
text_no:
Denne gamle opossumen kom inn. Jeg sa: "Se der, gutter, den opossumen har rabies." Jeg løp ut med en gang og tenkte: "Jeg bør stoppe den." Den kom rett mot meg. Jeg tok en rake og kastet den over opossumen. Jeg la merke til at beinet på venstre side var omtrent tre eller fire ganger så stort som et normalt opossumbein. Hundene hadde enten tygd på den, eller så hadde den blitt påkjørt av en bil. Det var mark overalt på den, der den holdt på å råtne med koldbrann, og det krydde av grønne fluer.
Jeg sa: "Å, her er det. Den er skadet, den har ikke rabies." Jeg holdt den under riven, en stor hagerive, mens den lå der. Herr Woods kom opp over feltet, og akkurat da kom melkebudet, herr Gilmore, inn gjennom porten.
Jeg sa: "Nå, gutter," til Gene og Leo, "kom hit. Jeg vil vise dere noe. Denne opossumen…" Jeg la merke til at en opossum og en kenguru er de eneste dyrene som har en lomme til å bære ungene sine i. Når nervene hennes reagerte på riven, slapp hun ned lommen, og der var ni små, nakne opossumunger som prøvde å die, mens hun beit etter riven.

text:
And I said, "Here's what it is. Looky here. She's a mother." I said, "Now, come here, Leo and Gene." I said, "Come here, I want to show you something. What I was just talking to you," I said, "this possum, this dumb brute here, is more of a mother than that colored girl is." I said, "She hasn't got thirty minutes to live, you can see she can't. She's dying now. But," I said, "she'll spend that thirty minutes fighting for them little naked babies." And I said, "That's mother love. That's the love that's in her heart for her babies." I said, "She will die for them babies."
And just then, Mrs. Woods, she's a veterinary. So she come by, and Mr. Woods. And I showed it to them, and Mr. Gilmore, five or six of us standing around looking. And so, I said…
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Se her. Hun er en mor." Jeg sa, "Kom hit, Leo og Gene. Jeg vil vise dere noe. Det jeg nettopp snakket om," fortsatte jeg, "denne opossumen, denne enkle skapningen, er mer av en mor enn den fargede jenta." Jeg sa, "Hun har ikke mer enn tretti minutter igjen å leve, det er tydelig. Hun er døende nå. Men," fortsatte jeg, "hun vil bruke de tretti minuttene på å kjempe for de små, nakne babyene sine." Jeg sa, "Det er morskjærlighet. Det er kjærligheten i hjertet hennes for babyene sine." Jeg sa, "Hun vil dø for disse babyene."
Akkurat da kom fru Woods, som er veterinær, forbi sammen med herr Woods. Jeg viste dem det, samt herr Gilmore og fem eller seks andre som sto rundt og så på. Jeg sa...

text:
Mrs. Woods said, "Well, Billy," said, "now the only thing to do is kill them." Said, "Because the possum has got a round mouth, you know," and said, "it can't nurse the bottle, they're too little anyhow." Said, "Now you just kill the mother, and then take the little ones and just hit them against the ground." Said, "They'll suffer like everything drinking the milk from her like that." Said, "She's dead. And I seen her biting on that."
I said, "She isn't dead."
Said, "But, she will die in a few minutes, you see that."
I said, "I just can't do it."
She said, "Why?"
I said, "I just don't know." I said, "I can't do it."
Said, "Well, you're a hunter aren't you?"
I said, "Yes, ma'am, but I'm not a killer." And so…
Said, "Well, why, don't you go get your gun and shoot her?"
I said, "I just can't do it."
"Well," said, "Why not?" Said, "Let Banks do it." That's her husband.
I said, "I just can't do it."
"Well," said, "Billy, you mean to tell me, you're going to let that old mother lay there like that, and them poor little babies drink that milk from her and die a horrible death?"
text_no:
Mrs. Woods sa, "Vel, Billy," sa hun, "det eneste du kan gjøre nå er å drepe dem." Hun sa, "Fordi opossum har en rund munn, vet du," og la til, "den kan ikke amme fra flaske, de er uansett for små." Hun fortsatte, "Nå dreper du bare moren, og så tar du de små og slår dem mot bakken." Hun sa, "De vil lide mye hvis de drikker melk fra henne nå." Hun sa, "Hun er død. Jeg så henne bite på det."
Jeg sa, "Hun er ikke død."
Hun sa, "Men hun vil dø om få minutter, det ser du."
Jeg sa, "Jeg klarer det bare ikke."
Hun spurte, "Hvorfor ikke?"
Jeg sa, "Jeg vet bare ikke." Jeg sa, "Jeg klarer det ikke."
Hun sa, "Vel, er du ikke jeger?"
Jeg sa, "Jo, frue, men jeg er ikke en morder." Og så...
Hun sa, "Vel, hvorfor går du ikke og henter geværet ditt og skyter henne?"
Jeg sa, "Jeg klarer det bare ikke."
"Vel," spurte hun, "Hvorfor ikke?" Hun sa, "La Banks gjøre det." Det er hennes mann.
Jeg sa, "Jeg klarer det bare ikke."
"Vel," sa hun, "Billy, mener du å si at du skal la den gamle moren ligge der slik, og la de stakkars små babyene drikke den melken fra henne og dø en grusom død?"

text:
I said, "Mrs. Woods, you being a doctor … or, veterinary, you know that that's the humane thing to do. But there's something in me," I said, "I can't do it." And I said, "She's wanting to let … she wants to stay with her babies till she dies." I said, "She must stay with her babies." So I let her up.
And when I did, she took off towards the house. And when she got right in front of my steps, she fell over. Said, "That's it. That's all of it." I said, "Well, she got to live a few more minutes with her babies."
And I went up there, and them little possums trying to nurse her. I punched her. She was exhausted. I tried to pour water on her. I finally got a little kind of the way they grin like. I said, "Well,…"
Said, "Why don't you kill the little ones?"
I said, "Just leave her alone."
Said, "Are you going to let her lay there like that, Billy?"
And I said, "Yes, ma'am."
So, all that day, we watched her; the little possums still trying to nurse. That night, Mr. Woods come out, said, "Billy, you've been going a long time now; let's take a little ride, get away from these crowd here." So, we went out riding.
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Mrs. Woods, du som er dyrlege, vet at det er det mest humane å gjøre. Men det er noe i meg," sa jeg, "jeg kan ikke gjøre det." Jeg fortsatte, "Hun ønsker å være med ungene sine til hun dør." Så jeg sa, "Hun må få bli med ungene." Og jeg lot henne gå.
Da jeg gjorde det, løp hun mot huset. Rett foran trappen falt hun sammen. Jeg sa, "Det var det. Nå er det over." Jeg sa, "Vel, hun fikk leve noen minutter til med ungene sine."
Jeg gikk bort til henne, og de små pungrottene prøvde å die henne. Jeg dyttet borti henne. Hun var utmattet. Jeg prøvde å helle vann på henne. Til slutt så jeg et lite smil fra henne. Jeg sa, "Vel,…"
Noen sa, "Hvorfor dreper du ikke de små?"
Jeg sa, "Bare la henne være i fred."
De spurte, "Skal du la henne ligge der, Billy?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja, frue."
Hele dagen så vi på henne; de små pungrottene fortsatte å prøve å die. Den kvelden kom Mr. Woods ut og sa, "Billy, du har vært her lenge nå; la oss ta en liten kjøretur, komme oss bort fra denne folkemengden." Så vi dro på en liten kjøretur.

text:
And that night, coming down the road, I seen a little old puppy laying on the side of the road. And I stopped, I went and got him. Oh, he was so full of mange, he stunk. And there was … so full of fleas and lice, till they was all over my hands. I brought him, put him in the car. My wife said, "You're not going to take that?"
I said, "Yes, honey." I said, "He's a little fellow. He ain't never had a chance to live."
She said, "Bill, you ain't going to take that thing home."
I said, "Sure, I'm going to pray for him, and God is going to let him get well." He's the prettiest big collie you ever seen today. Sure. Oh, sure. I believe that. I'm sure the dog… His picture will be in the Christian Businessmen's pretty soon, their magazine. So there he is, big fine collie. Prayer saved his life. He was a little fellow, he didn't have a right to die like that. Somebody dropped him off 'cause he had mange. God is the healer of animals the same as He is anything.
So, when we got in, about eleven o'clock, there the old possum was laying there. Now, Mrs. Woods, said, "Billy, now you know, she never moved." Said, "She's gone."
I said, "Well, that's right."
Said, "Let me go kill them possums."
I said, "No, no." We went in. Oh, that night, Billy come in (He had been fishing.) about twelve o'clock. Old possum still laying there.
text_no:
Den kvelden, da jeg kjørte nedover veien, så jeg en liten valp ligge på veikanten. Jeg stoppet og tok med meg valpen. Den var full av skabb og luktet vondt, samt dekket av lopper og lus som krøp over hendene mine. Jeg satte den i bilen. Min kone spurte: "Har du tenkt å ta med deg den?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja, kjære. Han er en liten stakkar som aldri har hatt en sjanse til å leve."
Hun sa: "Bill, du kan ikke ta med det dyret hjem."
Jeg svarte: "Jo, jeg skal be for ham, og Gud vil sørge for at han blir frisk." I dag er han den vakreste collien du noen gang har sett. Jeg tror fullt og fast på det. Hunden hans er nå avbildet i Christian Businessmen's magasin. Der er han, en stor, flott collie. Bønn reddet livet hans. Han var en liten stakkar, og hadde ikke rett til å dø på den måten. Noen hadde kastet ham fra seg på grunn av skabb. Gud er helbrederen av dyr like mye som av alt annet.
Da vi kom hjem, omtrent klokken elleve, lå den gamle opossumen der. Fru Woods sa: "Billy, hun har ikke beveget seg. Hun er død."
Jeg svarte: "Ja, det stemmer."
Hun fortsatte: "La meg drepe de små opossumene."
Jeg sa: "Nei, nei." Vi gikk inn. Den natten kom Billy inn rundt klokken tolv etter å ha vært på fisketur. Den gamle opossumen lå fortsatt der.

text:
Next morning about six, I got up, went out … six or seven. There the old possum laid there, dew all over her. I said, "Well, I…" And I happened to look standing behind there, I heard someone snubbing. It was my little girl Rebekah; tender-hearted little thing. And so, she was standing there, she said, "Daddy, that poor old mother. Is she dead?"
And I said, "I don't know, honey. Now, daddy will see." And I went out there and kind of shook her. I said, "I believe she is." I shook her with my foot again, and the little ones…
Said, "Is them little possums dead?"
I said, "No, they're still nursing." So, I shook her again like that. I finally seen that she was alive.
I said, "No, she's still alive."
She said, "Daddy, what are you going for her?" Said, "I dreamed that old possum all night."
I said, "Honey, I couldn't sleep either."
She said, "Well," said, "what are you going to do with her, daddy?"
I said, "I don't know, honey. I can't tell you."
Said, "Daddy, are you going to kill her?"
And I said, "No, honey, I'm not." I said, "I'm not going to kill her." I said, "You go on and get in the bed, sweetheart; it's too early for you to be up." And I said, "You go on now, and get in the bed a little while, and daddy come wake you up." And I kind of pushed her back like that, went on back, and I went into my room on the side, the den room, where all the animals that's in there. And, so I sat there, my head like this, kind of rubbing my head. And I thought, "My! What'll I do? I don't know what to do with that old possum." Many people has been healed right in that room. I was standing there like that. Many great visions come. And I said, "I don't know what to do with that thing."
text_no:
Neste morgen, rundt klokken seks eller syv, sto jeg opp og gikk ut. Der lå den gamle opossumen, dekket av dugg. Jeg sa: "Vel, jeg..." Da hørte jeg noen snufse bak meg. Det var min lille jente, Rebekah, den hjertevarme lille skapningen.
Hun sto der og sa: "Pappa, er den stakkars gamle moren død?"
Jeg svarte: "Jeg vet ikke, kjære. La pappa undersøke." Jeg gikk bort og ristet forsiktig i opossumen. Jeg sa: "Jeg tror hun er død." Jeg ristet henne igjen med foten, og de små...
Hun spurte: "Er de små opossumene døde?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, de dier fortsatt." Så ristet jeg henne igjen, og til slutt merket jeg at hun var i live.
Jeg sa: "Nei, hun lever fortsatt."
Hun sa: "Pappa, hva skal du gjøre med henne? Jeg drømte om den gamle opossumen hele natten."
Jeg svarte: "Kjære, jeg sov heller ikke."
Hun sa: "Vel, hva skal du gjøre med henne, pappa?"
Jeg svarte: "Jeg vet ikke, kjære. Jeg kan ikke si det."
Hun spurte: "Pappa, skal du drepe henne?"
Jeg sa: "Nei, kjære, det skal jeg ikke." Jeg sa: "Jeg skal ikke drepe henne. Gå tilbake til sengs nå, det er for tidlig for deg å være oppe." Jeg la til: "Gå og legg deg litt til, så kommer pappa og vekker deg senere." Jeg dyttet henne forsiktig tilbake. Deretter gikk jeg inn i rommet på siden, den lille stuen der alle dyrene er.
Jeg satt der med hodet i hendene og gned pannen. Jeg tenkte: "Hva skal jeg gjøre? Jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre med den gamle opossumen." Mange mennesker har blitt helbredet i det rommet. Jeg sto der, og mange store visjoner har kommet der. Jeg sa: "Jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre med henne."

text:
And a voice said to me, "Well, you preached your sermon about her yesterday, being a real mother." Now, not thinking what I was saying, or what that was, I said, "That's right. I was telling Leo and Gene about what a real mother she was."
Said, "You used her for a text."
And I said, "That's right. I did that. I used her for a text."
Said, "And she's laid at your door for twenty-four hours, waiting for her turn to be prayed for, like a lady. And you've never said a word to Me about her."
I said, "Well, I didn't even know that You…" Said, "Who am I talking to anyhow?" I looked around the room. My heart begin to jump. I said, "O God, You're here."
I knelt down, I said, "God, was that You talking to me? Where are you at, Father?"
text_no:
Og en stemme sa til meg: "Vel, du talte i går om henne som et eksempel på en ekte mor." Uten å tenke over hva jeg sa, svarte jeg: "Det stemmer. Jeg fortalte Leo og Gene om hvor enestående mor hun var."
Stemme sa: "Du brukte henne som et tekstgrunnlag."
Jeg svarte: "Det er sant. Jeg gjorde det, jeg brukte henne som et tekstgrunnlag."
Stemme sa: "Og hun har ligget ved din dør i tjuefire timer, ventet tålmodig på sin tur til å bli bedt for, som en ekte dame. Men du har ikke nevnt ett ord om henne til Meg."
Jeg sa: "Vel, jeg visste ikke engang at Du..." Stemme sa: "Hvem er det jeg snakker med uansett?" Jeg så meg rundt i rommet, hjertet mitt begynte å slå fortere. Jeg sa: "Å Gud, Du er her."
Jeg knelte ned og sa: "Gud, var det Du som snakket til meg? Hvor er Du, Far?"

text:
I didn't hear nothing. I run out to the old possum again. I looked down at her. And I said, "God, I… You mean that You put that dumb brute here? Why, I know that You direct all the sparrows, and You know all of them. I've seen You send a lot of people, but this is a dumb brute. This brute couldn't think, she hasn't got a soul. How did she get here? Did You send her here to be prayed for? Then if You did, Lord, forgive the stupidity of Your servant." I said, "I didn't mean to do that." I said, "Then, Lord God, I pray that You heal the possum, if You want her to live with her babies," And she come here, and that leg all chewed up, and laying back like that.
And I no more said that… Love had come to its end. Sovereign grace stepped in. That mother possum raised up on all four feet, looked at me, picked up those babies, and scooted them in the pocket, strutted right down that lane on all four feet, just as normally well as she ever was, a little twist in that tail.
Becky run out on the porch, said, "Daddy, is that the old possum?"
I said, "Jesus just healed her." And she went to the gate, turned around, looked at me, as if to say, "Thank you, kind sir." Strutted right over to the woods, and as far as I know is over there happy with her babies, tonight. Love. Hallelujah! Love of God, oh, how He does love.
text_no:
Jeg hørte ingenting. Jeg løp tilbake til den gamle possumen igjen. Jeg så ned på henne og sa: "Gud, betyr det at Du plasserte dette stumme dyret her? Jeg vet at Du styrer alle spurver og kjenner dem alle. Jeg har sett Deg sende mange mennesker, men dette er et stumt dyr. Dette dyret kan ikke tenke, hun har ingen sjel. Hvordan kom hun hit? Sendte Du henne hit for å bli bedt for? Da ber jeg om tilgivelse for Dine tjeners dumhet." Jeg sa: "Jeg mente ikke å gjøre det." Så sa jeg: "Da, Herre Gud, ber jeg at Du helbreder possumen, om Du ønsker at hun skal leve med ungene sine." Hun kom hit med det beinet helt oppspist og liggende slapt.
Jeg hadde knapt sagt det… Kjærligheten hadde nådd sitt ende. Suveren nåde trådte inn. Den moren possumen reiste seg på alle fire bein, så på meg, plukket opp ungene sine, og skyndte dem inn i lommen. Deretter struttet hun nedover stien på alle fire bein, like normal som hun noen gang hadde vært, med en liten vri på halen.
Becky løp ut på verandaen og sa: "Pappa, er det den gamle possumen?"
Jeg sa: "Jesus helbredet henne nettopp." Hun gikk til porten, snudde seg og så på meg, som om hun ville si: "Takk, snille herre." Deretter struttet hun rett over til skogen, og så langt jeg vet, er hun der borte lykkelig med ungene sine i kveld. Kjærlighet. Halleluja! Guds kjærlighet, å, hvor mye Han elsker.

text:
I told you the other night about the hunter, when that mother's love stood there on that doe, him squealing like a baby deer, me calling him a brute for doing it. That old mother doe run out there and yet, the hunter with a gun in his face. That doe never batted an eye. That love for that baby was still searching for it, and it brought that hunter under conviction. When he started to pull the trigger, he started quivering. I was standing behind him. He laid the gun down and throwed his arms around me, said, "Preacher, pray for me. Lead me to God. I can't help it no longer."
It's love. When you see the display of gallant love, you'll see the sovereign grace of God step in every time and take its place.
Here sometime ago, I was coming from Dallas. I was flying across home. I got in a storm up here over Memphis, the big plane come down, the TWA, and landed there. He put me in the Peabody Hotel. I couldn't afford to stay in there now. They put me in there for that night. And the next morning, they called me, and said, "Be down at the airport at seven o'clock. The limousine will pick you up."
Fixing to close now, listen close. Said, "Pick you up at seven o'clock."
text_no:
Jeg fortalte dere forleden kveld om jegeren som etterlignet et rådyrs skrik, og da moren kom til unnsetning, kalte jeg ham en villmann for å gjøre det. Den gamle råhjen løp rett ut, rett mot jegeren, med pistolen pekende mot seg. Råhia blunket ikke engang. Morens kjærlighet til sitt barn fikk henne til å fortsette å lete etter det, og det rørte jegeren dypt. Da han skulle til å trekke av, begynte han å skjelve. Jeg sto bak ham. Han la fra seg geværet, omfavnet meg og sa: "Forkynner, be for meg. Led meg til Gud. Jeg klarer det ikke lenger."
Det er kjærlighet. Når du ser et edelt kjærlighetsuttrykk, vil du alltid se Guds suverene nåde tre inn og ta sin plass.
For en tid tilbake, kom jeg fra Dallas. Jeg fløy hjemover da jeg kom inn i en storm over Memphis. Det store TWA-flyet landet, og de plasserte meg på Peabody Hotel. Jeg kunne ikke ha råd til å bo der nå, men for den natten ble jeg plassert der. Neste morgen ringte de og sa: "Vær på flyplassen klokken syv. Limousinen vil hente deg."
Nå skal jeg avslutte, så lytt nøye. De sa: "Vær klar klokken syv."

text: I said, "All right." Or they picked me up so I can be there at seven. Next morning, I got up real early, 'cause I'd come out from a meeting. I had some letters I'd wrote that night, and I thought, "I'll mail these first. The limousine will be along after awhile." So I walked out, and I said to the fellow, "Which way to the post office?"
text_no: Jeg sa: "Greit." De hentet meg slik at jeg kunne være der klokken syv. Neste morgen stod jeg opp veldig tidlig, ettersom jeg kom rett fra et møte. Jeg hadde skrevet noen brev kvelden før og tenkte: "Jeg sender disse først. Limousinen kommer snart." Så jeg gikk ut og spurte mannen, "Hvor er postkontoret?"

text:
He said, "Straight ahead, down that way." And I went out, started down there, I was going down the road singing:
They're gathered in the upper room,
All praying in His name,
Baptized with the Holy Ghost,
And power for service came;
Oh, what He did for them that day
He'll do for you the same,
I'm so glad that I can say I'm one of them.
Walking along down the street like that, singing it in my heart. All at once, Something said, "Stop!"
I thought, "That was just to impress me."
I said:
Oh come, my brother, seek this blessing
That'll cleanse your heart from sin,
Something said, "Stop!" There was a lot of fishing tackle there in the store. I got back in this place, like I was looking at fishing tackle, so nobody would watch me on the street. Busy right in there. And I got back in the corner.
I said, "Heavenly Father, was that You speaking to me?" I kept real quiet.
Said, "Turn and go back, and keep walking."
text_no:
Han sa: "Rett frem, nedover den veien." Jeg gikk ut og begynte å gå nedover veien mens jeg sang:
De er samlet i øvre rom,
Alle ber i Hans navn,
Døpt med Den Hellige Ånd,
Og kraft til tjeneste kom;
Å, det Han gjorde for dem den dagen
Vil Han gjøre for deg også,
Jeg er så glad at jeg kan si at jeg er en av dem.
Jeg gikk langs gaten og sang dette i mitt hjerte. Plutselig sa Noe: "Stopp!" Jeg tenkte: "Det var bare for å imponere meg." Jeg fortsatte å synge:
Å kom, min bror, søk denne velsignelsen
Som vil rense ditt hjerte fra synd,
Noe sa: "Stopp!" Der var det mye fiskeutstyr i butikken. Jeg gikk inn i butikken, som om jeg så på fiskeutstyret, slik at ingen skulle legge merke til meg på gaten. Jeg var opptatt der inne og kom meg til et hjørne.
Jeg sa: "Himmelske Far, var det Du som talte til meg?" Jeg holdt meg helt stille.
Han sa: "Snur deg og gå tilbake, og fortsett å gå."

text:
Do you believe in being led by the Spirit of God? I turned, started walking back, walked on past the hotel, just kept on walking. I looked at my watch, already seven. I missed the limousine. On down, on down, till I come way down there, in a little colored district, where there is colored people. Sun was way up high. I thought, Oh, I've missed my plane. But, Something just keeps telling me to walk." So I just kept on walking. That's the way. Don't question God. Do what He tells you to do. So, I just kept on walking.
And after awhile, I looked laying across the gate, and there was a typical old Aunt Jemima, with a man's shirt tied around her head, tears running down her cheeks. And I passed by, she said, "Good morning, Parson."
I said, "Good morning, Auntie." Walked on by.
I said, "Well, how… She said, 'Parson.'"
I turned back, I said, "Pardon me, a minute, Auntie." I said, "You called me a parson."
She said, "Yes, sir." And I said, "How did you know I was a parson?"
She said, "I knowed you was coming."
I said, "You knew I was coming?"
Said, "Yes, sir."
And she said, "I've been standing here since four o'clock." I looked on her.
And I said, "Well, bless your heart." She was wet on the back yet.
Said, "Yes, sir. I've been standing here." Said, "Did you ever read the story about the Shunammite woman?"
And I said, "Yes, ma'am."
Said, "I was that kind of woman." She said, "And I promised the Lord, if He'd give me a baby, I'd raise it for Him." And said, "He give me a fine boy."
text_no:
Tror du på å bli ledet av Guds Ånd? Jeg snudde, begynte å gå tilbake, gikk forbi hotellet og fortsatte å gå. Jeg så på klokken; den var allerede sju. Jeg hadde gått glipp av limousinen. Jeg fortsatte videre, og kom til slutt ned til et lite område hvor det bodde fargede mennesker. Solen var høyt oppe, og jeg tenkte: "Å, jeg har mistet flyet." Men noe fortsatte å fortelle meg å gå videre. Så jeg bare fortsatte å gå. Slik er det. Ikke still spørsmål ved Gud. Gjør det Han forteller deg å gjøre. Så jeg fortsatte å gå.
Etter en stund så jeg en typisk gammel tante Jemima, liggende over porten, med en mannskjorte bundet rundt hodet og tårer rennende nedover kinnene. Jeg gikk forbi, og hun sa: "God morgen, Pastor."
Jeg svarte: "God morgen, Tante." Og gikk videre.
Hun sa: "Pastor."
Jeg snudde meg og sa: "Unnskyld meg et øyeblikk, Tante. Du kalte meg pastor."
Hun svarte: "Ja, sir."
Jeg spurte: "Hvordan visste du at jeg var en pastor?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg visste at du kom."
Jeg spurte: "Du visste at jeg kom?"
"Ja, sir," sa hun.
Hun fortsatte: "Jeg har stått her siden klokken fire." Jeg så på henne.
Jeg sa: "Vel, velsigne ditt hjerte." Hun var fortsatt våt på ryggen.
Hun sa: "Ja, sir. Jeg har stått her." Så spurte hun: "Har du noen gang lest historien om kvinnen fra Sjunem?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja, ma'am."
Hun sa: "Jeg var en slik kvinne. Jeg lovet Herren at hvis Han ga meg et barn, skulle jeg oppdra det for Ham." Og sa: "Han ga meg en flott gutt."

text:
And she said, "Parson, I raised that boy, but when he got to be a man," said, "a young man, he got with some wrong company. And he done wrong." And said, "He got a bad disease, a venereal disease." And said, "He's laying there dying." And said, "Two days, he's been unconscious. The doctor man says, 'There's no hopes.'" Said, "We's good family here, we never thought of anything like that." And said, "He's dying," and said, "he's backslid." And she said, "Parson, I just can't stand to know my baby dying without knowing the Lord Jesus."
And I said, "What? Mother love."
She said, "I prayed for two days." And said, "This morning, about three o'clock, I was dreaming. And I dreamed I was talking to the Lord. And I said, 'Lord, where is Your Elijah?'"
And said, "I seen a man coming with a gray suit on with a little bitty semi-western hat." That's the way I was dressed.
text_no:
Hun sa, "Pastor, jeg oppdro den gutten, men da han ble voksen," sa hun, "ble han en ung mann og kom i dårlig selskap. Og han gjorde gale ting." Hun sa, "Han fikk en alvorlig sykdom, en kjønnssykdom." Hun fortsatte, "Han ligger der og dør." Hun sa, "Han har vært bevisstløs i to dager. Legen sier, 'Det er ingen håp.'" Hun sa, "Vi er en god familie her, vi hadde aldri trodd noe slikt skulle skje." Hun sa, "Han dør," og la til, "han har falt fra." Hun sa, "Pastor, jeg klarer ikke å bære tanken på at mitt barn skal dø uten å kjenne Herren Jesus."
Jeg sa, "For en morskjærlighet."
Hun sa, "Jeg har bedt i to dager." Hun fortsatte, "I morges, rundt klokken tre, drømte jeg. Jeg drømte at jeg snakket med Herren. Jeg sa, 'Herre, hvor er Din Elia?'"
Hun sa, "Jeg så en mann komme med en grå dress og en liten halv-vestlig hatt." Det var slik jeg var kledd.

text:
Said, "He said, 'Just wait.' And said, "I walked right out here, and been standing here ever since. I knowed you were coming." Said, "Now, I seen you coming." Said, "I thought, 'Lord, You stop him. I won't have to say a thing.'"
Now with all that, the Holy Spirit telling me to walk, all that in my heart, I thought, "This must be it, Lord." Then about eight.
I said, "Well, Auntie," I said, "My name is Branham."
She said, "I's glad to know you, Parson Branham."
I said, "Did you ever hear of me?"
Said, "No, sir, I don't believe I ever did."
I said, "My ministry is praying for the sick." I don't think she was along them lines.
But she said, "No, sir, I never did hear of you."
And I said, "The Holy Spirit had me walking down this way."
She said, "Won't you come in?"
And when I went in, they had an old white-washed fence, and a plow-point hanging on the gate. When I walked in to that little old colored … where colored people live, there wasn't nothing there, but a little… No rug on the floor, just wood like this. And a little old poster bed. But a sign hanging on the wall, "God bless our home."
I'd rather have that than all the pinups, or anything else, that you could put in there. That showed that that was a Christian home.
text_no:
""Han sa, 'Vent litt.' Så sa han, "Jeg gikk rett ut her, og har stått her siden. Jeg visste at du skulle komme." Han sa, "Nå, jeg så deg komme." Han fortsatte, "Jeg ba, 'Herre, stopp ham. Jeg trenger ikke å si noe.'"
Med alt dette, og Den Hellige Ånd som fortalte meg at jeg skulle gå, tenkte jeg, "Dette må være det, Herre." Da var klokken omtrent åtte.
Jeg sa, "Vel, Tante," og introduserte meg, "Jeg heter Branham."
Hun svarte, "Hyggelig å møte deg, Pastor Branham."
Jeg spurte, "Har du hørt om meg?"
Hun svarte, "Nei, sir, jeg tror ikke det."
Jeg sa, "Min tjeneste er å be for de syke." Jeg tror ikke hun kjente til det.
Hun sa igjen, "Nei, sir, jeg har aldri hørt om deg."
Jeg fortsatte, "Den Hellige Ånd ledet meg hit."
Hun sa, "Vil du ikke komme inn?"
Da jeg gikk inn, så jeg et hvitmalt gjerde og en plogskjær som hang på porten. Jeg gikk inn til huset hvor de fargede bodde. Det var ingenting der, bortsett fra ... Ingen teppe på gulvet, bare treplanker. En gammel plakat-seng og et skilt på veggen som sa, "Gud velsigne vårt hjem." Jeg ville heller ha det enn alle plakater eller noe annet man kunne henge opp. Det viste at dette var et kristent hjem."

text:
Great big fine boy there, about … looked like about eighteen. Had the blanket in his hand, going "Hm-hm-hm-hm." Said, "It's dark; it's dark here."
I said, "What's the matter with him, can't he talk?"
Said, "No, he's been unconscious, two days." Said, "He thinks he's out in a big deep sea, and he's in a darkness and lost." The tears running down her big old fat cheeks. She said, "That's it, Parson. I can't stand to hear my baby die, and have that on my heart the rest of my days, that my baby was lost."
text_no:
En stor, flott gutt der, cirka ... så ut som omtrent atten år gammel. Han holdt et teppe i hånden og sa "Hm-hm-hm-hm." Han sa, "Det er mørkt; det er mørkt her."
Jeg spurte, "Hva er galt med ham, kan han ikke snakke?"
Hun svarte, "Nei, han har vært bevisstløs i to dager." Hun sa, "Han tror han er ute på et stort dypt hav, og han er i mørket og fortapt." Tårene rant nedover hennes store, gamle, fyldige kinn. Hun sa, "Det er sant, pastor. Jeg orker ikke høre mitt barn dø, og leve resten av mine dager med vissheten om at mitt barn var fortapt."

text:
And I thought, "Baby?" Weigh a-hundred-and-eighty pound. Now, but, that was a mother's love. No matter what he'd done, he was still baby. He was still her loving child. I looked at her. I couldn't hardly choke back the tears, looking at her. And I said, "Is he very bad?"
Said, "He's dying, Parson." Said, "The doctor man said don't call him no more. He's gone."
I felt his feet, real sticky. Now I don't say, I… Felt like, you know, a person gets that real cold, sticky. And his feet felt cold. I said, "Well, I guess maybe he is."
So then, he just kept pulling this. And so, I said, "Let us pray, Auntie."
And she got down there, looked over at me, and I got at the foot of the bed and held the boy by the feet. I said, "Auntie, will you lead us in prayer?"
She said, "Yes, Parson."
Oh, brother, you talk about a prayer. I just cried like a baby. To hear that old saint, and it was just quiet and cool.
Said, "Lord God," she said, "last night when You spoke to me, Your poor handmaid, in the dream, and told me that this parson was coming, I knowed that my baby's going to speak to me, and say he's saved before he goes." And she started like that, the tears running down both of our cheeks then. When she got through praying, she reached down and got her apron, wiped her tears.
And she said, "Now, will you pray, Parson?"
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "Baby?" Kunne jo veie ett hundre og åtti pund. Men det var en mors kjærlighet. Uansett hva han hadde gjort, var han fortsatt hennes baby, hennes elskede barn. Jeg så på henne, og det var vanskelig å holde tilbake tårene. Jeg spurte: "Er han veldig syk?"
Hun svarte: "Han er døende, Pastor. Legen sa vi ikke skulle ringe ham igjen, han er borte."
Jeg kjente på føttene hans, de var veldig klamme og kalde. Det føltes som den kalde, klamme følelsen man får ved døden. Jeg sa: "Vel, da er han kanskje det."
Han fortsatte å bevege seg litt, og jeg sa: "La oss be, Tante."
Hun satte seg ned, så på meg, og jeg gikk til fotenden av sengen og tok gutten i føttene. Jeg sa: "Tante, kan du lede oss i bønn?"
Hun svarte: "Ja, Pastor."
Å bror, du skulle hørt bønnen hennes. Jeg gråt som en baby. Å høre den gamle helgenen, det var helt stille og kjølig.
Hun ba: "Herre Gud," sa hun, "i går natt da Du talte til meg, Din fattige tjenestekvinne, i drømmen og fortalte at denne pastoren skulle komme, visste jeg at mitt barn ville tale til meg og si at han er frelst før han går."  Tårene rant nedover våre kinn. Da hun var ferdig med å be, tok hun forkleet og tørket tårene.
Hun sa: "Nå, kan du be, Pastor?"

text:
I said, "Yes, ma'am." I put my hand over on the boy, I said, "God, my plane's gone. I don't know why, but You told me to walk. This must be the case. I pray, God, that You'll be merciful to this boy, somehow the sovereign love of this woman praying for her baby. You brought me down here."
Just then I hear him go "Mmm!" Said, "Oh, mammy!"
She said, "Yes, honey."
Said, "It's getting light around here now." Said, "I'm nearing the shore." In a few minutes, he was sitting up on the bed.
About six months from then, I was going down, somewhere in the South. I went in on a train. They wanted about seventy-five cents for a hamburger on the train. I get it, about fifteen cents there at the station. I just waited till it pulled in at Memphis. And as you get off the train, you know how you walk up to that little restaurant. I was going walking along there that morning. I had got on at Louisville that night and started down there. I heard somebody say, "Hello, Parson Branham."
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Ja, frue." Jeg la hånden min på gutten og sa: "Gud, flyet mitt har gått. Jeg vet ikke hvorfor, men Du ba meg gå. Dette må være grunnen. Jeg ber, Gud, at Du viser miskunn mot denne gutten. Denne kvinnen ber for sitt barn med inderlig kjærlighet. Du førte meg hit."
Akkurat da hørte jeg at han sa: "Mmm!" og så: "Å, mamma!"
Hun svarte: "Ja, kjære."
Han sa: "Det blir lysere her nå." Så: "Jeg nærmer meg stranden." Noen minutter senere satt han oppreist i sengen.
Omtrent seks måneder senere var jeg på vei sørover. Jeg tok toget. De ville ha omtrent syttifem cent for en hamburger på toget, men jeg kunne få den for rundt femten cent på stasjonen. Så jeg ventet til vi kom til Memphis. Da jeg gikk av toget, vet du hvordan man går opp til den lille restauranten. Jeg var på vei dit den morgenen. Jeg hadde gått på i Louisville kvelden før og startet reisen. Plutselig hørte jeg noen si: "Hei, Pastor Branham."

text:
I looked around, there stood a little red cap out there.
Said, "How are you, Parson Branham?"
I said, "How do you do, son!" I said, "How did you know me?"
He said, "You don't know me, do you?" Said, "You remember that morning, that you come down to pray for me? My mammy here, you know, had been out at the gate waiting for you."
I said, "Are you the boy?"
Said, "Yes, sir, Parson Branham." Said, "I's not only healed, but," said, "I's a Christian now."
What was it? That morning, when I got back to my plane, I jumped in a cab as soon as I left that house, and got back, run to the station to see what train … what plane I could get next. They said, "Last call," for this certain plane. God, by the love of that poor, ignorant, colored woman, her love for God and her baby, had grounded a plane and held it there. Hallelujah!
When love is projected, sovereign grace steps in and takes its place. God knew His gift. God knowed what would happen. God had selected this to be so. And the love of that mother had held… The love of that poor, ignorant colored woman, probably didn't know her ABC's; but she knowed of the love of God. That's what grounded that airplane, and held it three hours.
When I got on, I said, "What happened, hostess?"
She said, "Oh, there was something happened somewhere else, or something." They held it there.
I thought, "Well, I know it was. It happened in Glory." Amen.
text_no:
Jeg så meg rundt, og der stod det en liten gutt med rød lue. Han sa: "Hvordan går det, Pastor Branham?"
Jeg svarte: "Hvordan har du det, gutt! Hvordan kjente du meg igjen?"
Han sa: "Du kjenner meg ikke, gjør du vel? Husker du den morgenen da du kom for å be for meg? Min mor her har ventet ved porten på deg."
"Er du den gutten?" spurte jeg.
"Ja, Pastor Branham," svarte han. "Jeg er ikke bare helbredet, men jeg er en kristen nå."
Hva var det som skjedde? Den morgenen, da jeg kom tilbake til flyet, hoppet jeg inn i en taxi så snart jeg forlot huset, og dro til stasjonen for å finne ut hvilken flyvning jeg kunne ta videre. De sa: "Siste opprop" for en bestemt flyvning. Gud, gjennom kjærligheten til denne fattige, uvitende, fargede kvinnen, hennes kjærlighet til Gud og hennes barn, hadde holdt tilbake flyet. Halleluja!
Når kjærlighet blir projisert, trer suveren nåde inn og tar dens plass. Gud visste om Sin gave. Gud visste hva som ville skje. Gud hadde utvalgt dette til å være slik. Og kjærligheten til denne moren hadde holdt... Kjærligheten til denne fattige, uvitende, fargede kvinnen, som kanskje ikke en gang kunne sitt ABC; men hun kjente Guds kjærlighet. Det var det som holdt tilbake flyet i tre timer.
Da jeg gikk om bord, spurte jeg: "Hva skjedde, vertinne?"
Hun svarte: "Å, det var noe som skjedde et annet sted, eller noe." De holdt tilbake flyet.
Jeg tenkte: "Vel, jeg vet hva det var. Det skjedde i Æren." Amen.

text:
I tell you, brother, there's nothing like the love of God. Do you love Him, tonight? Is He your Saviour? Have you got love that you can project to Him? That in your dying hours, sovereign grace. Yes, I've got to come to the end of the road some day, that's true brother. One of these nights, I'll preach my last sermon. I'll close this Bible for the last time. And I know, probably … I hope I'm an old man. Perhaps, a few gray beards hanging around. But, I'm leaning on the staff, that I come to the end of the road. Oh, my!
I want to look back down through every briar patch and every hill, everywhere my footprints has been; I hope it's ground for Jesus. When I know I've fought the last hour, I know I've sung the last song, I've prayed my last prayer, I've preached my last sermon, as I'm standing on the banks of the Jordan, the old breakers is dashing against my soul, the doctors walk away, the saints stand with bowed heads, and I feel the breakers coming in on my soul.
I want to take the helmet off, lay it down on the river bank. I want to unbuckle the sandals and slip them off. I want to take the sword and stick it back in the sheaf of eternity. Raise both hands up. Say, "Send out the lifeboat, Lord. I'm coming Home this morning." Don't worry, He'll be there. He'll be waiting. I want to live for Him now. That in that hour, when I go down through the valley of the shadow of death, I want two glossy wings of the Holy Spirit to bear me over the river. He'll be there, if you'll just trust in Him.
text_no:
Jeg sier deg, Bror, det finnes ingenting som Guds kjærlighet. Elsker du Ham i kveld? Er Han din Frelser? Har du kjærlighet som du kan vise Ham? Når dine siste timer kommer, vil suveren nåde være der. Ja, en dag må jeg også nå reisens ende, det er sant, Bror. En av disse nettene vil jeg holde min siste preken. Jeg vil lukke Bibelen for siste gang. Og jeg vet, antageligvis... Jeg håper jeg er en gammel mann. Kanskje, med noen få grå skjegg rundt. Men jeg støtter meg på staven når jeg kommer til veiens ende. Åh, min!
Jeg vil se tilbake gjennom hver tornebusk og hver høyde, overalt hvor mine fotspor har vært. Jeg håper det er grunn for Jesus. Når jeg vet at jeg har kjempet min siste time, sunget min siste sang, bedt min siste bønn, og holdt min siste preken, mens jeg står ved bredden av Jordan-elven, gamle bølger slår mot min sjel, legene går bort, mens helgenene står med bøyde hoder, og jeg føler bølgene komme inn over min sjel.
Jeg vil ta hjelmen av, legge den ned på elvebredden. Jeg vil løsne sandalene og ta dem av. Jeg vil sette sverdet tilbake i evighetens slire. Løfte begge hendene. Si, "Send ut livbåten, Herre. Jeg kommer Hjem i morges." Ingen grunn til bekymring, Han vil være der. Han vil vente. Jeg vil leve for Ham nå. For i den timen, når jeg går gjennom dødsskyggens dal, vil jeg at Den Hellige Ånds to glinsende vinger skal bære meg over elven. Han vil være der, hvis du bare stoler på Ham.

text:
Let us speak to Him, now.
Our Heavenly Father, as we are here in this great revival, in the great Shreveport, these dear southern people, Lord. Kind, and nicest I've ever met in my life. Humble, even to the sinners. Seems to be such a lovely people. Not saying it before them, Lord, I'd be a hypocrite to pray like that. Just for them, I'm talking to You, Lord. See many of them still in sin, still in darkness, still they don't know the love of God. They don't know the loving Lord Jesus and what, really many of them are trusting, because they belong to church. Many of them are believing that they'd be saved at that day, because that they're in good standing with their neighbor, or with their pastor, or with the church. Many of them think because they know the Bible real well.
O Christ, I'm persuaded tonight, that except a man is born again, except his whole nature, his whole lookout, that temper, that indifference towards Christ… It's changed, it's humbled, brought down to the sweetness, brought down to a place to where God can lead.
text_no:
La oss tale med Ham nå. 
Himmelske Far, vi står her i denne store vekkelsen i Shreveport, sammen med disse kjære sørlige menneskene, Herre. De er vennlige, de hyggeligste jeg har møtt i mitt liv.
